content.text: Pretraining is the foundational stage that transforms a randomly-initialised neural network into a model that understands language, code, and world knowledge. Everything that makes a large language model useful — its ability to reason, write, summarise, and assist agents — is absorbed during this single, enormously expensive phase before a single line of fine-tuning is applied.

Pretraining is the process of initialising a large neural network with random weights and then optimising those weights over an enormous corpus of raw text using self-supervised learning. No human labels are needed. The training signal comes entirely from the text itself: given the tokens seen so far, the model must predict the next token. After hundreds of billions to trillions of such predictions the model internalises the statistical structure of language — grammar, facts, reasoning patterns, coding conventions, and much more.

. It is not yet useful as a conversational assistant (it will simply continue any text fed to it), but it contains rich representations that every downstream fine-tuning stage builds upon. Skipping or shortcutting pretraining degrades every capability that follows.
The core task: causal language modelling
, the model is trained to maximise the log-likelihood of each token given all preceding tokens. The cross-entropy loss minimised during training is:

are updated via gradient descent to reduce this loss across the training corpus. Because the task is self-supervised, the corpus can be arbitrarily large — the internet, books, code repositories, scientific papers — without requiring any annotation effort.

For practitioners building agentic AI systems, pretraining is the single most important leverage point. The capabilities that make an agent useful — reading comprehension, multi-step reasoning, code generation, tool-use patterns, commonsense inference — are overwhelmingly determined by what was learned during pretraining. Fine-tuning shapes behaviour and safety; pretraining installs the underlying knowledge.
A base model trained on 2 trillion tokens has implicitly compressed billions of facts into its weights. No retrieval system alone can match this for zero-shot breadth.
Because strong priors are already present, downstream tasks (instruction following, function calling, domain-specific reasoning) converge far faster and with far fewer labelled examples.
Capabilities like multi-step arithmetic, in-context learning, and chain-of-thought reasoning appear at scale thresholds without being explicitly trained — they emerge from the pretraining objective.
Agents that must plan, use tools, and recover from errors draw directly on the commonsense and causal reasoning absorbed during pretraining. A weak base model cannot be rescued by prompting alone.
The next-token objective requires no human annotation, enabling training sets of unprecedented scale.
The same pretrained model can be fine-tuned for summarisation, translation, coding, medical reasoning, and agentic tool use.
Strong pretraining unlocks in-context learning: the model can solve new tasks from a handful of examples in the prompt without any weight update.
One pretraining run produces a checkpoint that can be specialised into dozens of downstream products, amortising the enormous compute cost.
Facts and relationships are learned from data rather than hand-engineered, making the model adaptive to the statistical distribution of the internet.
Training a 70B-parameter model on 2T tokens requires millions of GPU-hours and tens of millions of dollars. Only organisations with significant resources can pretrain from scratch.
Everything learned is frozen at the end of pretraining. The model has no awareness of events after its data cutoff without retrieval augmentation or continual learning.
Internet-scale data contains bias, misinformation, and harmful content. Imperfect filtering means some of it enters the model's priors.
Loss spikes, NaN gradients, and hardware failures can derail multi-week runs. Robust checkpoint management and fault tolerance are critical.
A base model predicts next tokens — it does not follow instructions or refuse harmful requests. Alignment stages (SFT, RLHF) are necessary before deployment.
The pretraining loop has five stages that repeat billions of times:
Raw text is converted to integer IDs using a learned tokeniser (Byte-Pair Encoding or SentencePiece unigram). Vocabulary sizes typically range from 32 000 to 128 000 tokens.
Token sequences are concatenated and split into fixed-length chunks (e.g. 4 096 tokens) to fill each training example fully, maximising GPU utilisation.
Each packed chunk is fed through the transformer. The model produces a probability distribution over the vocabulary for every position.
Cross-entropy loss is computed by comparing predicted probabilities to the actual next token at each position. The loss is averaged over the batch.
Gradients are backpropagated through the full model. The AdamW optimiser updates weights using the gradient and its exponential moving averages.
This cycle repeats for every mini-batch. A training run spanning 2 trillion tokens with a batch size of 4 million tokens requires roughly 500 000 gradient steps.

The learning rate follows a warmup-then-cosine-decay schedule. During the warmup phase (typically a few thousand steps) the learning rate rises linearly from near-zero to the peak value. Then it follows a cosine curve down to roughly 10% of peak at training end. This prevents early instability and allows fine-grained convergence near the end of training.

Modern LLMs universally use a decoder-only transformer. The encoder-decoder design (used in early models like T5) fell out of favour for causal language modelling because decoder-only models are simpler, require no cross-attention, and scale more efficiently. Key architectural choices made at the start of pretraining:
Replaces absolute positional encodings. RoPE encodes relative distances in the attention mechanism and generalises better to longer context windows than sinusoidal or learned absolute positions.
Key and Value projections are shared across groups of query heads. This cuts KV-cache memory by 8x or more while barely affecting quality — critical at inference time for long contexts.
The gated linear unit variant of the FFN outperforms ReLU and GELU at the same parameter count. Used in Llama-2/3, Mistral, and most open-weight models.

Each transformer block applies layer normalisation before the attention and FFN sub-layers (pre-norm). RMSNorm drops the mean-centering component of LayerNorm, reducing compute slightly while maintaining stability.
A lower-triangular mask ensures each position can only attend to previous tokens, enforcing the autoregressive property required for next-token prediction.
The Chinchilla paper (Hoffmann et al., 2022) established that compute-optimal training requires scaling model size and token count proportionally. Given a compute budget
(in FLOPs), the optimal number of parameters

In practice, many open-weight models overtrain relative to Chinchilla-optimal (i.e. they use more tokens than optimal for the parameter count) because inference cost matters more than training cost in deployment — a smaller model trained on more tokens costs less to serve.
As a practitioner building agentic systems, you will almost never pretrain a model from scratch. The decision tree is:
if your domain is covered by the public corpus (general English, common code languages, science). Fine-tune instead.
if you have a large domain-specific corpus (millions of documents) poorly represented in the base model's training data. Run additional pretraining steps on your corpus before fine-tuning.

only if you need: (a) a unique language or script not in existing models, (b) extreme data-distribution requirements such as proprietary financial tick data or low-level assembly, or (c) model ownership requirements that prohibit using any existing checkpoint.

Understanding pretraining mechanics is valuable even when you do not run it yourself, because it explains why models behave the way they do — their knowledge cutoffs, their biases toward common internet phrasing, their emergent reasoning capabilities, and their failure modes on edge cases.
The following example shows a minimal but complete pretraining setup using PyTorch and the Hugging Face ecosystem. It covers data loading, model initialisation, training loop, and checkpointing.

# minimal_pretrain.py  — illustrative, not production-ready
import torch
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, IterableDataset
from transformers import (
    AutoTokenizer,
    LlamaConfig,
    LlamaForCausalLM,
    get_cosine_schedule_with_warmup,
)
from torch.optim import AdamW
from itertools import islice

# 1. Tokeniser
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf")
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

# 2. Model (small config for illustration)
config = LlamaConfig(
    vocab_size=tokenizer.vocab_size,
    hidden_size=2048,
    intermediate_size=5504,
    num_hidden_layers=16,
    num_attention_heads=16,
    num_key_value_heads=8,   # GQA: 8 KV heads for 16 query heads
    max_position_embeddings=4096,
    rope_theta=10000.0,
)
model = LlamaForCausalLM(config).cuda()
print(f"Parameters: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters()) / 1e9:.2f}B")

# 3. Streaming dataset — reads text, tokenises, packs into fixed-length chunks
CHUNK = 4096

class PackedTextDataset(IterableDataset):
    """Streams raw text lines, tokenises, packs into CHUNK-length sequences."""
    def __init__(self, path: str):
        self.path = path

    def __iter__(self):
        buffer = []
        with open(self.path) as f:
            for line in f:
                ids = tokenizer(line.strip(), add_special_tokens=False)["input_ids"]
                ids.append(tokenizer.eos_token_id)
                buffer.extend(ids)
                while len(buffer) >= CHUNK + 1:
                    chunk = buffer[: CHUNK + 1]
                    buffer = buffer[CHUNK + 1 :]
                    input_ids = torch.tensor(chunk[:-1], dtype=torch.long)
                    labels    = torch.tensor(chunk[1:],  dtype=torch.long)
                    yield {"input_ids": input_ids, "labels": labels}

dataset = PackedTextDataset("corpus.txt")
loader  = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=4)

# 4. Optimiser + cosine-with-warmup scheduler
TOTAL_STEPS  = 100_000
WARMUP_STEPS = 2_000
optimiser = AdamW(
    model.parameters(), lr=3e-4, betas=(0.9, 0.95), weight_decay=0.1
)
scheduler = get_cosine_schedule_with_warmup(
    optimiser, WARMUP_STEPS, TOTAL_STEPS
)

# 5. Training loop
model.train()
for step, batch in enumerate(islice(loader, TOTAL_STEPS)):
    input_ids = batch["input_ids"].cuda()
    labels    = batch["labels"].cuda()

    outputs = model(input_ids=input_ids, labels=labels)
    loss = outputs.loss

    loss.backward()
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)
    optimiser.step()
    scheduler.step()
    optimiser.zero_grad()

    if step % 500 == 0:
        print(f"step={step:6d}  loss={loss.item():.4f}  "
              f"lr={scheduler.get_last_lr()[0]:.2e}")

    if step % 5_000 == 0 and step > 0:
        model.save_pretrained(f"checkpoints/step-{step}")
        tokenizer.save_pretrained(f"checkpoints/step-{step}")

If you have a domain corpus (medical, legal, scientific) and want to adapt an existing model without full pretraining, domain-adaptive pretraining resumes training from an existing checkpoint on your corpus for a modest number of additional steps:

# domain_adaptive_pretrain.py
from transformers import (
    AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer,
    TrainingArguments, Trainer, DataCollatorForLanguageModeling
)
from datasets import load_dataset

BASE_MODEL = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B"

# Load existing checkpoint — all pretrained weights are preserved
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(BASE_MODEL)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    BASE_MODEL, torch_dtype="bfloat16"
)

# Stream a domain corpus; here a small subset for illustration
dataset = load_dataset("text", data_files={"train": "biomedical_corpus.txt"})

def tokenise(example):
    return tokenizer(
        example["text"],
        truncation=True,
        max_length=4096,
    )

tokenised = dataset["train"].map(
    tokenise, batched=True, remove_columns=["text"]
)

collator = DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(
    tokenizer=tokenizer, mlm=False  # causal LM, not masked
)

args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="./llama3-biomedical-base",
    per_device_train_batch_size=2,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=8,   # effective batch = 16
    learning_rate=1e-4,              # lower than from-scratch; preserves priors
    lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
    warmup_steps=500,
    max_steps=20_000,
    bf16=True,
    logging_steps=100,
    save_steps=2_000,
    dataloader_num_workers=4,
)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model, args=args,
    train_dataset=tokenised, data_collator=collator
)
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model("./llama3-biomedical-base/final")
The following runnable example demonstrates raw base model behaviour — the pure "text continuation" capability that exists immediately after pretraining, before any instruction fine-tuning:

# inspect_base_model.py — probe base model behaviour before fine-tuning
from transformers import pipeline, AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

# Small open-weight model; freely downloadable without a gated token
MODEL = "EleutherAI/pythia-410m"

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(MODEL, torch_dtype=torch.float32)

generator = pipeline(
    "text-generation",
    model=model,
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
    device=-1,  # CPU
)

demos = [
    # Factual continuation
    "The capital of France is",
    # Code continuation
    "def fibonacci(n):\n    if n <= 1:\n        return n\n    return",
    # Arithmetic
    "17 multiplied by 43 equals",
    # In-context few-shot (the model infers the pattern)
    "English: cat\nFrench: chat\nEnglish: dog\nFrench:",
]

for prompt in demos:
    result = generator(
        prompt,
        max_new_tokens=25,
        do_sample=False,
        pad_token_id=tokenizer.eos_token_id,
    )
    completion = result[0]["generated_text"][len(prompt):].strip()
    print(f"PROMPT     : {prompt!r}")
    print(f"COMPLETION : {completion!r}")
    print()

# Expected (approximate):
# 'The capital of France is' -> 'Paris.'
# 'def fibonacci ...'        -> 'fibonacci(n-1) + fibonacci(n-2)'
# '17 multiplied by 43 ...'  -> '731'
# 'English: dog\nFrench:'   -> 'chien'
#
# None of these are explicitly labelled tasks. They emerge from
# next-token prediction across a vast internet-scale corpus.
(Meta, 2024) is the most documented large-scale pretraining run in the open-weight ecosystem. Key facts:
15 trillion tokens from a curated mixture of web crawl (CommonCrawl), code, multilingual data, books, and scientific papers. Quality filtering was aggressive — less than 5% of crawled pages passed.
The 405B model required approximately 30.8 million GPU-hours of H100 time. At public H100 cloud pricing, this exceeds $60 million.
Decoder-only transformer with 126 layers, 16 384 hidden size, 128 attention heads, grouped query attention (8 KV heads), RoPE, SwiGLU FFN, and a 128 256 token vocabulary.

Three phases — initial pretraining at 4 096-token context, then a long-context phase extending to 131 072 tokens using RoPE scaling, then an annealing phase with upweighted high-quality data at low learning rate.
The pretrained checkpoint became the foundation for Llama 3 Instruct, Meta's agentic tooling, and hundreds of community fine-tunes. A single pretraining run enabled dozens of downstream use cases.

The context length baked in during pretraining is very hard to extend later. If you pretrain at 2 048 tokens and later need 32 768, you must perform expensive long-context continued pretraining or use approximations like RoPE scaling. Decide your context needs before the pretraining run.

The quality of training data dominates quantity at equal compute budgets. A model trained on 1T high-quality tokens routinely outperforms one trained on 2T dirty tokens. Invest in deduplication (MinHash LSH), language identification, heuristic quality filtering, and domain balancing before starting a run.

Distributed training jobs fail. Hardware failures, network partitions, and preemption are routine on thousand-GPU runs. Checkpoint at least every 1 000 steps and store on persistent storage — not local SSD that is lost when a pod is killed.
Learning rate too high or no warmup

Large learning rates cause loss spikes that can permanently damage training. A common sign is the loss suddenly jumping from 2.3 to 5+ and not recovering for thousands of steps. Use gradient clipping (max norm 1.0), keep the peak learning rate below 3e-4, and always include a warmup phase.
Training in FP32 instead of BF16

FP32 training wastes roughly 2x the memory and 2x the compute relative to BF16. Use BF16 for the forward and backward pass — it has wider dynamic range than FP16 and avoids NaN gradients without loss scaling. Keep a master copy of weights in FP32 for the optimiser update only.

Use the Chinchilla compute-optimal formula to determine the right model size and token count before locking in architecture and data pipeline choices. Small ablations (1B parameters, a few billion tokens) can validate assumptions cheaply.

Track gradient norm, activation statistics, and downstream eval benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, ARC) throughout training. Loss alone can hide subtle quality regressions caused by data contamination or numerical issues.
prevents the model from memorising training examples, leading to better generalisation and lower held-out perplexity.

The final 1-5% of training steps, when the learning rate is at its minimum, have outsized impact on final quality. Upsample your best data sources (books, curated Q&A, high-quality code) during this phase.
Train the tokeniser on the full corpus before starting model training. A poorly balanced vocabulary (e.g. undersegmenting rare languages) harms both training efficiency and cross-lingual ability.
Plan for fault tolerance from day one.
Implement automatic checkpoint resumption, loss anomaly detection that triggers alerting, and a runbook for restarting from the last good checkpoint.
Pretraining a modern LLM requires distributed training across hundreds or thousands of accelerators. Three types of parallelism are combined:
Each GPU holds a full copy of the model and processes a different micro-batch. Gradients are averaged across GPUs using all-reduce. Most efficient when the model fits on a single device.

Individual weight matrices are sharded across GPUs along one dimension. Each GPU computes a partial matrix multiply and results are gathered via all-reduce inside the layer. Requires fast intra-node interconnect (NVLink).

Groups of transformer layers are assigned to different GPUs. Each stage processes one micro-batch while the next stage processes the previous micro-batch, creating a pipeline. Careful scheduling is needed to avoid pipeline bubbles.
Intermediate activations are discarded during the forward pass and recomputed on demand during the backward pass. This halves activation memory at a roughly 30% throughput cost.
A hardware-aware attention kernel that avoids materialising the full
attention matrix in HBM. Reduces attention memory from
Shards optimiser states, gradients, and model parameters across data-parallel ranks, eliminating the memory duplication inherent in standard data parallelism.
ZeRO optimisation, mixed precision, CPU offloading
Tensor and pipeline parallelism at scale
NVIDIA; used in production LLM training
Hugging Face; used to build the FineWeb dataset
Dao-AI-Lab; 2-4x throughput gain over naive attention
Fast BPE and Unigram tokeniser training
Hugging Face; Rust-backed, handles large corpora
Behaviour/format shaping; thousands of labelled examples
Dynamic knowledge; no weight updates needed

An adversary who can inject text into a public crawl can attempt to backdoor a model during pretraining. Research shows that even a small fraction of poisoned documents (as low as 0.01% of the corpus) can introduce exploitable behaviours that survive through fine-tuning. Mitigations include cryptographic provenance tracking for data sources, heuristic anomaly detection on newly added crawl data, and holding out validation sets from independent crawls to detect unexpected distribution shifts.

LLMs memorise verbatim sequences from training data, particularly repeated documents. Personal data, API keys, credentials, and private emails present in the crawl can be extracted via targeted prompts. Mitigations: aggressive deduplication (removing documents that appear more than a few times greatly reduces extractable memorisation), PII scrubbing pipelines before tokenisation, and targeted evaluation to quantify memorisation risk before releasing a checkpoint.

Pretrained checkpoints are extremely valuable assets. An attacker who obtains a checkpoint from a run that was never intended for public release gains full access to the model's capabilities, including any residual harmful content not removed by later alignment. Treat checkpoint storage with the same security posture as production secrets: encryption at rest, strict access control, and audit logging.

A single hardware failure can corrupt a gradient accumulation step and propagate silently for hundreds of steps before becoming visible as anomalous loss. Implement loss monitoring with automatic anomaly alerts, checksum verification on checkpoint restoration, and loss curve auditing at resume points to detect silent corruption early.
universal compression of human knowledge into weights

. The model is a lossy compressor that learns to predict any text in the corpus; to do so, it must represent grammar, facts, world models, coding idioms, and reasoning strategies as efficiently as possible in its parameters. The weights are not a lookup table — they are a compact set of statistical priors that can be interpolated and composed to solve problems the model never saw verbatim during training.

: it teaches the model to surface the right compressed knowledge for specific tasks in the right style. Without a strong pretrained foundation, fine-tuning has nothing useful to route through — this is why pretraining is the irreducible core of all LLM capability, and why the quality of the base model sets the ceiling for everything that follows.
Language understanding, reasoning, and world knowledge are absorbed here. Fine-tuning shapes how that knowledge is expressed, not whether it exists.
This self-supervised task is the engine of all capability — no human labels required, just enormous amounts of raw text.
Aggressive filtering, deduplication, and domain balancing have a larger effect on final model quality than simply adding more raw text.
Compute-optimal training (Chinchilla) allows you to predict model performance before running a full training job, enabling rational resource allocation.
Domain-adaptive pretraining or fine-tuning from an open-weight base is the practical path for most teams.
Architecture choices made at pretraining are sticky.
Context length, positional encoding scheme, vocabulary, and attention variant are expensive to change after the run. Decide deliberately upfront.
Security must be built in from the start.
Data poisoning, memorisation of sensitive content, and checkpoint protection cannot be retroactively applied — they must be part of the pretraining design.
